title: Melanoma — Diagnosis and Management
text: Melanoma is a malignant tumour of melanocytes. Cutaneous melanoma most often arises de novo in normal-appearing skin, although approximately 25% develop within a pre-existing melanocytic naevus. Major clinical subtypes include superficial spreading melanoma (the most common form, comprising about 70% of cases), nodular melanoma (rapidly growing, often amelanotic), lentigo maligna melanoma (chronically sun-exposed skin in older adults), and acral lentiginous melanoma (palms, soles, subungual). The clinical diagnosis is guided by the ABCDE rule: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variation, Diameter greater than 6 mm, and Evolution over time. Dermoscopy increases diagnostic accuracy and may reveal atypical pigment network, irregular streaks, blue-white veil, regression structures, and atypical vascular patterns. Definitive diagnosis requires excisional biopsy with 2 mm clinical margins, including subcutaneous fat. Histopathology determines Breslow thickness (the strongest prognostic factor), ulceration, mitotic rate, lymphovascular invasion, and microsatellitosis. Wide local excision margins follow AJCC guidelines: 0.5 cm for in situ, 1 cm for tumours up to 1 mm thick, and 2 cm for tumours over 2 mm thick. Sentinel lymph node biopsy is recommended for Breslow thickness greater than 0.8 mm or for thinner lesions with adverse features. Adjuvant therapies for stage III and IV disease include immune checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1 monotherapy or combined anti-PD-1/anti-CTLA-4) and BRAF/MEK inhibitors for BRAF-V600 mutated tumours.

title: Basal Cell Carcinoma — Clinical Features and Treatment
text: Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) is the most common malignant skin cancer worldwide and arises from the basal layer of the epidermis or follicular structures. Chronic ultraviolet (UV) exposure is the principal aetiological factor; additional risk factors include fair skin (Fitzpatrick I–II), age greater than 50 years, immunosuppression, and inherited syndromes such as Gorlin syndrome. Clinically, several morphological variants are recognised: nodular BCC presents as a pearly papule with telangiectasia and a rolled border, often with central ulceration (the classic rodent ulcer); superficial BCC appears as a scaly erythematous patch on the trunk and may mimic eczema; morphoeic (sclerosing) BCC presents as an ill-defined indurated white plaque with subclinical extension; pigmented BCC may be confused with melanoma. Dermoscopy reveals arborising vessels, leaf-like areas, blue-grey ovoid nests, spoke-wheel structures, and ulceration. Confirmation requires punch or shave biopsy. Treatment selection depends on histological subtype, size, location, and patient factors. Standard surgical excision with 4 mm margins yields cure rates above 95% for low-risk lesions. Mohs micrographic surgery is recommended for high-risk anatomical sites (central face, ears, periocular region), recurrent tumours, and aggressive histological subtypes. Non-surgical options include cryotherapy and curettage for low-risk superficial lesions, topical imiquimod 5% or 5-fluorouracil for superficial BCC, photodynamic therapy, and vismodegib or sonidegib (Hedgehog pathway inhibitors) for locally advanced or metastatic disease.

title: Cutaneous Squamous Cell Carcinoma
text: Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC) is the second most common skin cancer and arises from malignant proliferation of epidermal keratinocytes. Major risk factors include cumulative UV exposure, fair skin phototype, age, immunosuppression (notably solid organ transplant recipients), chronic wounds and scars (Marjolin ulcer), human papillomavirus infection, and exposure to arsenic or ionising radiation. cSCC most commonly develops on chronically sun-exposed skin: the face, scalp, dorsa of hands, forearms, and lower lip. Clinically, lesions present as an indurated hyperkeratotic papule, plaque, or nodule that may ulcerate and bleed. Bowen disease (cSCC in situ) appears as a slowly enlarging erythematous scaly plaque. Tenderness, rapid growth, and a diameter greater than 2 cm are concerning features. High-risk cSCC is defined by Brigham and Women's Hospital staging or AJCC criteria and includes tumours greater than 2 cm, depth beyond subcutaneous fat or greater than 6 mm, perineural invasion, poor histological differentiation, and location on the ear, lip, or temple. Treatment of choice is surgical excision with 4–6 mm margins for low-risk lesions or Mohs micrographic surgery for high-risk tumours. Adjuvant radiotherapy is considered for perineural invasion or positive margins not amenable to re-excision. Cemiplimab (anti-PD-1) is approved for locally advanced and metastatic disease.

title: Actinic Keratosis (Solar Keratosis)
text: Actinic keratosis (AK), also termed solar keratosis, is a partial-thickness intraepidermal dysplasia of keratinocytes and is considered a precursor to cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma. AK typically develops on chronically sun-exposed skin in fair-skinned individuals after middle age. Lesions present as rough, scaly, erythematous macules or papules, often more easily felt than seen, on the scalp (in balding men), face, ears, dorsa of hands, and forearms. The lifetime risk of progression of a single AK to invasive SCC is low (estimated 0.025–16% per year per lesion), but field cancerisation (multiple confluent lesions in a sun-damaged area) confers higher cumulative risk. Dermoscopy in AK reveals a strawberry pattern with erythematous pseudo-network around dilated follicular openings filled with yellowish keratotic plugs. Management depends on the number and distribution of lesions. Isolated lesions are treated with cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen) or curettage. Field-directed treatments include topical 5-fluorouracil, imiquimod, ingenol mebutate, diclofenac, photodynamic therapy with methyl aminolevulinate, and chemical peels. Sun protection with broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreens, protective clothing, and avoidance of midday UV exposure is essential for both treatment efficacy and prevention of new lesions.

title: Melanocytic Naevi (Moles) — Clinical Atlas
text: Melanocytic naevi (moles) are benign neoplasms or hamartomas composed of melanocytes. They are subclassified by depth of melanocyte nests into junctional (at the dermoepidermal junction), compound (junctional and dermal components), and intradermal (purely dermal) naevi. Congenital melanocytic naevi are present at birth or arise during infancy and are categorised by projected adult size: small (less than 1.5 cm), medium (1.5–20 cm), large (20–40 cm), and giant (greater than 40 cm). Large and giant congenital naevi confer increased lifetime melanoma risk and may be associated with neurocutaneous melanosis. Acquired melanocytic naevi appear in childhood and adolescence, peak in number in the third decade, and tend to involute thereafter. The atypical (dysplastic) naevus has clinical features overlapping with melanoma: diameter greater than 5 mm, irregular borders, variegated colour, and ill-defined edges. The presence of more than 100 melanocytic naevi or more than 5 atypical naevi is associated with elevated melanoma risk and warrants regular dermatological surveillance with dermoscopy and total-body photography. Dermoscopic patterns of benign naevi include reticular, globular, homogeneous, and parallel-furrow (acral) patterns. Excision is indicated for lesions with atypical features, change over time, or symptoms such as bleeding.

title: Seborrheic Keratosis — Benign Epidermal Proliferation
text: Seborrheic keratosis (SK) is the most common benign epidermal tumour, typically arising after the age of 40 on the trunk, face, scalp, and upper extremities. SK presents as a well-demarcated, waxy or verrucous papule or plaque with a stuck-on appearance and colours ranging from yellow-brown to dark brown or black. Lesions may be solitary or numerous and are not related to UV exposure. Dermoscopy shows characteristic features: comedo-like openings (keratin-filled plugs), milia-like cysts (white round structures), fissures and ridges (gyri and sulci pattern), fingerprint-like areas, and sharply demarcated borders without atypical pigment network. The major differential diagnosis is pigmented basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, and pigmented actinic keratosis; ambiguous lesions warrant biopsy. The eruptive appearance of multiple SKs (Leser-Trélat sign) has been associated with internal malignancy, although this association is debated. Treatment is cosmetic and includes cryotherapy, curettage, shave excision, electrodesiccation, or topical 40% hydrogen peroxide solution. Recurrence is uncommon when complete removal is achieved.

title: Psoriasis — Diagnosis and Treatment Overview
text: Psoriasis is a chronic immune-mediated inflammatory skin disease driven by aberrant activation of T helper 17 cells and tumour necrosis factor alpha. The most common form, plaque psoriasis, presents as well-demarcated erythematous plaques with adherent silvery-white scale, classically distributed over extensor surfaces (elbows, knees), the scalp, lumbosacral region, and umbilicus. Other clinical variants include guttate psoriasis (acute onset following streptococcal pharyngitis, predominantly in children and young adults), inverse psoriasis (intertriginous areas without prominent scale), pustular psoriasis (sterile pustules, may be generalised and life-threatening), and erythrodermic psoriasis. Nail involvement (pitting, oil-drop sign, subungual hyperkeratosis, onycholysis) occurs in up to 50% of patients and is associated with psoriatic arthritis. Up to 30% of patients develop psoriatic arthritis with peripheral, axial, dactylitic, or enthesitic patterns. Diagnosis is primarily clinical; biopsy is reserved for atypical presentations and shows parakeratosis, neutrophilic Munro microabscesses, regular acanthosis, and thinning of suprapapillary plates. Treatment is staged by disease severity using the body surface area, Psoriasis Area and Severity Index (PASI), and Dermatology Life Quality Index (DLQI). Mild disease responds to topical corticosteroids, vitamin D analogues (calcipotriol), tar, and tazarotene. Moderate to severe disease requires phototherapy (narrowband UVB) or systemic agents including methotrexate, ciclosporin, acitretin, and apremilast. Biologic therapies targeting TNF-alpha (adalimumab, etanercept), IL-17 (secukinumab, ixekizumab), IL-23 (guselkumab, risankizumab), and IL-12/23 (ustekinumab) have transformed treatment of severe disease.

title: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)
text: Atopic dermatitis (AD), commonly known as eczema, is a chronic relapsing inflammatory skin disorder characterised by pruritus, xerosis, and a typical morphology and distribution. Pathogenesis involves epidermal barrier dysfunction (frequently due to filaggrin loss-of-function mutations), type 2 immune dysregulation, and altered cutaneous microbiome with Staphylococcus aureus colonisation. AD usually begins in infancy: facial and extensor distribution in infants, flexural involvement in children, and lichenified plaques at flexures and on hands in adolescents and adults. The Hanifin and Rajka criteria require pruritus plus three or more of: typical morphology and distribution, chronic or relapsing course, personal or family history of atopy, and supporting features including xerosis, ichthyosis, keratosis pilaris, IgE reactivity, hand or foot eczema, cheilitis, recurrent conjunctivitis, Dennie-Morgan infraorbital fold, and early age of onset. Severity is assessed with SCORAD, EASI, or POEM scales. Management is built on a stepped approach: daily emollient use and trigger avoidance (irritants, allergens, sweat, heat); topical corticosteroids of appropriate potency for flares; topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus) for sensitive areas; antihistamines for sleep; and treatment of secondary bacterial or viral infections. Moderate to severe disease may require phototherapy (narrowband UVB) or systemic immunosuppressants (ciclosporin, methotrexate, azathioprine). Targeted biologics — dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα), tralokinumab (anti-IL-13), and oral JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib, abrocitinib) — have markedly improved outcomes in refractory disease.

title: Rosacea — Phenotypes and Treatment
text: Rosacea is a chronic inflammatory facial dermatosis characterised by transient or persistent centrofacial erythema, telangiectasia, papules, pustules, and in advanced cases phymatous change. The condition usually begins in adulthood (third to fifth decade) and is more common in fair-skinned individuals of northern European descent. Triggers include UV exposure, heat, alcohol, spicy foods, hot beverages, emotional stress, and certain medications. The 2017 phenotype-based classification recognises diagnostic features (persistent centrofacial erythema, phymatous changes) and major features (flushing, telangiectasia, inflammatory papules and pustules, ocular manifestations). Subtypes recognised in earlier classifications include erythematotelangiectatic, papulopustular, phymatous, and ocular rosacea. Differential diagnosis includes acne vulgaris (presence of comedones favours acne), seborrhoeic dermatitis (scaling in scalp and nasolabial folds), and lupus erythematosus. Treatment is targeted to the predominant phenotype: persistent erythema and flushing respond to topical brimonidine or oxymetazoline (alpha-adrenergic agonists) and vascular laser; papulopustular disease is treated with topical metronidazole, ivermectin, or azelaic acid, and oral doxycycline at sub-antimicrobial doses; phymatous disease responds to oral isotretinoin and surgical or laser reduction; ocular rosacea benefits from lid hygiene, artificial tears, and oral tetracyclines. All patients require daily broad-spectrum sunscreen and trigger avoidance.

title: Vitiligo — Diagnosis and Repigmentation Therapy
text: Vitiligo is an acquired autoimmune disorder characterised by selective destruction of cutaneous melanocytes, resulting in well-demarcated depigmented macules and patches. The condition affects approximately 0.5–2% of the worldwide population without sex or ethnic predilection, although the impact is often more visible and psychologically distressing on darker skin tones (Fitzpatrick IV–VI). Two clinical patterns are recognised: non-segmental vitiligo (the most common, bilateral and symmetric, with progressive course) and segmental vitiligo (unilateral, dermatomal-like distribution, typically rapid onset followed by stabilisation). Associated autoimmune conditions include autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto thyroiditis, Graves disease), type 1 diabetes mellitus, alopecia areata, pernicious anaemia, and Addison disease; thyroid function should be screened. Wood lamp examination accentuates depigmentation and aids diagnosis of subtle lesions. Treatment goals are repigmentation and stabilisation. First-line options include potent topical corticosteroids and topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus) for limited disease; narrowband UVB phototherapy or targeted excimer laser (308 nm) for more extensive involvement; topical JAK inhibitors (ruxolitinib 1.5% cream is approved for non-segmental vitiligo); and surgical techniques (suction blister grafting, melanocyte-keratinocyte cell suspension) for stable segmental disease. Photoprotection is essential because depigmented skin is susceptible to sunburn and actinic damage.
